column7: message

> Glad you agree. Sadly, according to many, it is not. 

Being against the stickers does not mean you are against the notion.

> Oh ridiculous. This is the kind of dehumanizing and reductionist belief that we need to avoid if we're ever going to cooperate. This is the original sin of today's extreme left. People are so much more than the color of their skin and to reduce people down like that and then make assumptions like that while totally ignoring things like, you know, personal history, is preposterous and dangerous. 

Hey you know what's preposterous and dangerous? Pretending that race doesn't impact a person's life.

> On an institutional level, maybe.

Fun fact: Institutions are made up of...*individuals*.

> Looking at individuals and saying 'you're privileged because of factor X!' isn't helping. It's incredibly divisive, in fact. 

Yeah yeah I am well aware that white people feel they need to be coddled when it comes to racial subjects.

> This is the mistake being made by today's left. They conflate groups with individuals. They can't seem to separate them. 

All white people benefit from privilege. All of them.

> You are not your race. It's amazing to me that it is the left today that needs to be told this. 

But your race does impact how society sees you and interacts with you. It's ridiculous to say otherwise.

> It is also an incredibly convenient way to avoid talking about privileges that are orders of magnitude more important and predictive, like wealth. I'm not wealthy, but if I were, I'd be laughing myself all the way to the bank. 

Woah now! People are *not* their socioeconomic statuses. I can't believe you would conflate groups with individuals like this!

I mean are you *actually* going to sit here and tell me that a factor largely beyond your control like your socioeconomic status at the time of your birth affects your life? What about the individual? What about not conflating groups with individuals?! Now all of a sudden you want to sing the song of privilege?

I wonder why that is - could it be that because you're actually not privileged when it comes to socioeconomic status you can recognize the privilege you lack? Could it be that you actually do see yourself as part of a group and are able to recognize that society sees you differently because you're a member of that group?

It's always hilarious to me when "today's right" hem and haw about privilege but then try and bring up wealth disparity. I mean you seriously don't see the irony do you? "Don't conflate groups with people! But also have we talked about poor people and the struggles they, as a group, face?"

Give me a break.

I can't say I can work up much fake vitriol upon reading "It is Ok to be white".  However, if I was pressed to make a wild guess as to the person who put up the poster I would say, racist or troll.  We all know that we never go around stating the obvious, we let the obvious speak for itself, like someone once said "the lady doth protest too much, methinks".
I would be interested in your view of Black Lives Matter, kettle meet  pot, or something entirely different, please clarify

I just wanna say, it's cool to talk about these things openly. I mean, everyone should feel good about who they are, right? About BLM, I think it's important because it highlights issues that need to be talked about. Not trying to start a fight, just saying what I think.

column7:
Differences in DNA. There are quite a lot of differences in DNA between people of different ethnicities 

I understand your experiment. 

Unfortunately, we haven't met any aliens yet.

How about socioeconomic status as a common trait?  It would make sense to me that people who are poor are more inclined to be desperate.   People who are desperate are more inclined to commit crimes.   IF poor people are dis-proportionally black, then that might explain violent crimes being dis-proportionally committed by black people.  I'm not saying this is the case, I'm just theorizing here.  If this is the case, then it makes sense people thinking that black people commit more crimes because they are black...when in actuality it may just be because they are poor.  


A poor black person and a poor white person, are equally as desperate and therefore equally as dangerous.  


I'll take the thought process a bit further.  If we can prove what I'm saying above, then we can do something about it.  We can work on how to get people out of poverty.  But if the stats show that black people do not commit more crime then white people, or we can't agree that poverty is the real issue, then we won't be follow that path as a solution.
"@urbane-sequence I'm not trying to argue DNA doesn't play a role, but let's focus on what we can change now. Poverty seems like a tangible issue we can address to help reduce crime overall."
"@odious-dailies Thanks for clarifying your perspective. Let's keep the discussion focused on actionable solutions and avoid attributing behaviors to any specific group. Keep it constructive."

column7: Grappling with crippling depression is also a "biological imperative" by some definition. It doesn't mean that we should make it a matter of policy to admit everyone with clinical depression into the service.

It is arguable that choosing a gender is a biological imperative too.

Porque no los dos?

column7: That's only partially true. It is believed to be caused by hormone levels, just not hormone levels during the living and breathing part of life. Excessive or insufficient hormonal influence while developing as a fetus is believed to be the primary culprit. There are also strong correlations with male/female bodied individuals with hormonal insensitivies that identify as transgender and this becomes a somewhat chicken vs. egg problem. Did the hormonal insensitivity cause them to develop a brain that was gendered incorrectly in the womb or did they develop hormonal insensitivity because they did not receive a correct starting dose of that hormone in the womb?

>Scientists currently believe that gender dysphoria is caused by either excessive hormones or a hormone insensitivity.

Not true. I'ts been disproven that trans people are trans because of abnormal hormone levels, in fact before hrt it's the norm to have hormonal levels perfectly congruent with your birth sex.

Now, that doesn't mean gender isn't biologically based, I just wanted to correct that line because that's not what scientists believe about gender dysphoria.

I understand where you're coming from, but it seems like there’s still a lot we don’t fully understand about gender identity. The science is evolving, and it’s important to keep an open mind. Let’s not dismiss any theories outright.
